segments.s531-g89: This clause has a somewhat archaic word order: a numeral/indefinite (یکی) + adjective (پاک) + noun (صندوق) followed by the verb with an attached object pronoun (کردش). The enclitic -ش on کردش refers back to صندوق and means “it”; کرد here is the simple past verb “made/did,” so the phrase says he made the chest black (کردِ + pronoun = “made it”).
segments.s531-g90: بدو = be + ضمیر (to it/him) introduces the beneficiary/target (here ‘to it’ = into the chest). اندرون ساخته is a perfective construction: ساخته (made) follows اندرون (inside/within) and describes that he built or formed an inner compartment; جایگاه is the noun ‘place/holding’ that names what was made. Together: “to it he made an inner place.”
segments.s531-g91: چنان شد که is a standard result/introduction clause meaning “so it came to pass that / such happened that.” در شهر is the locative phrase “in the city,” and بی‌هفتواد is a descriptive epithet “the kinless” attached to شهر. Word order places the circumstance before the following statement introduced by که.
segments.s531-g92: This clause states the resulting situation: نگفتی سخن کس is an idiomatic classical phrasing meaning ‘no one spoke a word’ (سخن = word/speech; کس = anyone). به بیداد و داد specifies the topic ‘concerning injustice and (also) justice.’ Note the absence of an explicit negative verb; the whole construction functions to assert that no speech on those matters was heard.
segments.s531-g93: فراز آمدش places the adverbial فراز (“on high/upwards” or poetically “aloft/above”) before the verb آمد with enclitic -ش = ‘to him,’ so ‘came to him on high’ (i.e., were exalted or bestowed upon him). ارج و آزرم و چیز are coordinated nouns (‘honor and reverence and things/possessions’), the objects/contents of what came to him.
segments.s531-g94: توانگر شد is a simple past/resultative ‘became wealthy.’ In classical Persian the verb often precedes its noun subject, so آن هفت فرزند (those seven children) follows the verb; نیز at the end adds ‘also/too.’
segments.s531-g95: یکی + noun introduces ‘a certain …’ (یکی میر = a certain mir/commander). بد is an adjective ‘bad’ modifying میر. اندر آن شهراوی is the locative phrase ‘in that townsman/town’ (شهراوی = of the town/townsman), so the whole gives ‘a bad mir in that town.’
segments.s531-g96: سرافراز is an adjective/adverb meaning ‘highly honored/lofty/triumphant’ describing the mir’s bearing. با + an itemized list (لشکر و رنگ و بوی) lists what accompanies him: با لشکر = ‘with an army,’ رنگ = ‘appearance/colour/splendor,’ and بوی = ‘scent’ (here idiomatic for fragrance or pomp). The coordination implies pomp and show accompanying the commander.
segments.s531-g105: هرانجا که بایست دینار داد is a relative/adverbial clause: هرانجا که (wherever, literally “every place that”) introduces the location/condition; بایست is an archaic/modal past form meaning “was necessary/one ought to” (like باید), governing the infinitive/verb that follows; دینار داد literally ‘a dinar was to be given’ where داد is the main verb in past tense meaning ‘was given’ or here elliptical for ‘needed to be paid’. Word order places the condition first (هرانجا که) and the necessity + object after it.
segments.s531-g106: به کنداوران چیز بسیار داد is a simple past clause with agent omitted (he). به marks the indirect object ‘to/for’; کنداوران is the plural of کنداور (here ‘excavators/diggers’); چیز بسیار is a noun phrase ‘many/a great many things’ (بسیار = ‘many/a lot’ modifying چیز); داد is the past verb ‘gave’. The clause therefore states the action performed for the diggers.
segments.s531-g107: یکی لشکری شد بر او انجمن is a nominal/verb cluster meaning ‘a certain army came into being against him’ (یکی = ‘a certain’/‘one’; لشکری = ‘military force/army’ used as the noun that ‘شد’ affects). شد is the past-tense verb ‘became/was formed’; بر او marks the target ‘against him’; انجمن is an appositive noun ‘assembly’ describing the gathered group. Classical word order allows apposition and the predicate ‘شد’ to appear before/among the nouns.
segments.s531-g108: همه نامداران شمشیرزن is a noun phrase in apposition describing that assembly: همه (‘all/everyone’) + نامداران (‘the renowned ones’) + شمشیرزن (‘swordsmen’). The copula ‘were’ is omitted (typical in Persian), so the phrase reads as ‘all the renowned ones (were) swordsmen.’
segments.s531-g109: همه یکسره پیش فرزند اوی is an adverbial phrase setting time/manner and place: همه یکسره = ‘all at once/altogether’ or ‘they all straightway’; پیش = ‘before/in the presence of’; فرزند اوی = ‘his son’ (اوی is an archaic possessive meaning ‘his’). The whole phrase modifies the following verb برفتند.
segments.s531-g110: برفتند و گشتند پیکارجوی contains two coordinated past verbs: برفتند (‘they went/they proceeded’) + و (‘and’) + گشتند (‘they became/turned’) followed by the predicate noun پیکارجوی (‘fighters/warriors’). The sequence means they went (to him) and then took on the role of combatants; گشتند is copulative here (‘became’).
segments.s531-g25: Adjective + numeral + possessed noun + copula: گرامی (an epithet) modifies یکی دخترش. یکی precedes the possessed noun دخترش and بود is the past copula 'was'. و بس is a short predicate meaning 'and that was enough' or 'and that sufficed'.
segments.s531-g26: The clause begins with the conjunction/relative که introducing the consequence or explanatory clause. نشمردی is a past/irrealis form of شماردن 'to count' (here 'one would not/count could not count'); او refers back to the person (he), دختران را is the definite/direct object marked by را, and به کس means 'for/to any person' (i.e. 'to anyone').
segments.s531-g27: چنان ... که is a correlative/consequence construction ('so ... that'). روزی here is an adverbial 'one day' (poetic use). همه همگروه is the subject phrase 'all the companions' (همه = all, همگروه = fellow-members/companions).
segments.s531-g28: نشستند is the past 3rd-person-plural of نشستن meaning 'they sat' / 'they gathered' (here 'sat together' or 'assembled'). با introduces accompaniment 'with', دوک 'spindle' is the instrument/thing they hold, در پیش = 'in front of' / 'before' (پیش = in the presence of), and کوه = 'the mountain'.
segments.s531-g29: برآمیختند is past 3rd-plural 'they mixed' or 'they blended' (object implied). آن is a pronoun 'it' or 'that', کجا is 'where', and داشتند is past 'they had'—so 'they mixed it where they had it/where it was.'
segments.s531-g30: به گاه is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of / when', خورش is the cooked dish or 'stew', دوک is again 'spindle', and بگذاشتند (ب + گذاشتند) is the past 'they put/they laid down'—here 'they put down the spindle' at mealtime.
segments.s531-g31: Again the correlative چنان ... که introduces a resultant scene: این دختر نیک‌بخت is the noun phrase 'this lucky/fortunate girl' (این = this, دختر = girl/daughter, نیک‌بخت = fortunate), functioning as the topic of the following event.
segments.s531-g32: یکی here functions as 'one/once'; سیب 'apple' is the affected item; افگنده is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'fallen' or 'having been cast down'; باد is a poetic/archaic particle often expressing optative or a past-tense/existential flavor (here part of the verbal phrase meaning 'fell/was fallen'); از = 'from', درخت = 'tree'. The whole gives 'one apple (had) fallen from the tree.'
segments.s531-g65: ازان پنبه — a prepositional phrase 'from that cotton' (ازان = 'from that', followed by the noun پنبه 'cotton'). هرچند introduces a concessive adverbial 'however much / although' that qualifies the following verb phrase. کردی فزون is a 2nd-person past construction: کردی (you did/made) with فزون functioning adverbially as 'more/increasingly' — literally 'you made it more'. The whole segment is an adverbial concessive clause that sets up the condition for the main clause that follows.
segments.s531-g66: برشتی is an archaic/poetic past form (3rd‑person singular) of bereštan meaning 'sprout/grow/become' or otherwise 'came to be'; here it functions as the main verb of the clause. همی is the persistent aspect particle 'still/continually' placed before the subject. دختر پرفسون is the subject phrase 'the maiden P̣arfuson' (دختر = 'maiden'); پرفسون is an uncertain/rare name/epithet and is treated as a proper name here. Grammatically: همی + verb + subject order is a poetic inversion emphasizing continuity of the action.
segments.s531-g67: چنان بد که is a formulaic sequence introducing a result or narrative continuation: چنان = 'so/such', بد here functions idiomatically ('so it was that') and که introduces the clause. یک روز = 'one day' (time phrase). مام و پدر = 'mother and father' (مام is a dialectal/poetic 'mother'). Word order here is typical narrative: temporal phrase after the connective, then the people who act.
segments.s531-g68: بگفتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural 'they said/spoke'. با before a person ('با دختر پرهنر') marks the addressee/partner: 'to/with the daughter'. دختر پرهنر = 'the skilled/accomplished daughter' (پرهنر = 'full of skill'). The verb precedes the prepositional/addressee phrase, a common word order in epic Persian.
segments.s531-g69: که introduces reported speech/what they said. چندین = 'several/many a' and modifies the following noun بریسی (an uncertain or dialectal term here; its exact meaning is unclear in the text). مگر in interrogative contexts functions as a modal/rhetorical 'perhaps / is it that' (not its basic 'except' sense), so the phrase reads 'چندین X مگر ...' = 'perhaps several X ...'. با پری = 'with a fairy' (با = 'with/by', پری = 'fairy/beautiful supernatural woman') offering an alternative reading (did you take many X, or rather a fairy?).
segments.s531-g70: گرفتستی is 2nd‑person past perfect/preterite 'you took/you had taken' (an action attributed to the addressee). ای is the vocative 'O' addressing the woman. پاک تن is an epithet 'pure-bodied' (پاک = 'pure/clean', تن = 'body'). خواهری here is a form of address 'sister' or 'sisterly one' (vocative); the phrase together is a respectful address 'O pure-bodied sister'. The clause is a direct question embedded in the parents' speech.
segments.s531-g71: سبک سیم تن is a poetic descriptive phrase: سبک = 'light' (of build or movement) and سیم تن literally 'silver-bodied' (a compound epithet describing appearance). This whole phrase is an adverbial/manner description of the speaker and precedes the reporting verb. پیش مادر = 'before/in the presence of her mother' (پیش = 'in front of / before / in the presence of'), and بگفت is the past 'she said / she told'. The order places the descriptive phrase up front for emphasis, then the scene (in the mother's presence) and the reporting verb.
segments.s531-g72: ازان سیب و آن کرمک اندر نهفت is a reported content clause of what she told her mother. ازان = 'of/from that' or 'that' pointing back to something already mentioned; سیب = 'apple'; و آن کرمک = 'and that little worm' (کرمک is a diminutive 'little worm'); اندر = 'inside/in' and نهفت is the past participial/archaic past 'hidden, concealed' (here meaning 'she had hidden [them] inside'). Grammatically this is a simple past clause reporting concealment.
segments.s531-g81: مر is a dative/emphatic 'to me' placed before the object for emphasis; این کرم را is the definite object phrase ('this worm' + object marker را). خوار نگذاشتند uses نگذاشتند (past negative of گذاشتن in the sense 'to allow/let') with the adjective خوار to mean 'did not allow (it) to be despised/treated as worthless.' The whole clause therefore means 'they did not let this worm be despised (in my presence/for me).'
segments.s531-g82: بخوردنش is a deverbal/nominal form meaning 'its being eaten' or here more naturally 'their feeding it'; نیکو is an adverb/adjective 'well/good'; همی is an archaic adverb 'continually/ever(so)'; داشتند is the past of داشتن used as a habitual/continuous auxiliary here ('kept' / 'were keeping' it well). Together: 'they kept feeding it well.'
segments.s531-g83: تن‌آور شد is an adjectival predicate + شد, 'became strong-bodied' (شد = 'become'). آن کرم is the subject following the verb (poetic inversion). و links the next predicate; نیرو گرفت uses گرفت in the sense 'to take/receive' so 'gained strength.'
segments.s531-g84: سر و پشت او is a coordinated noun phrase 'head and back (of him/it)' where او is the 3rd‑person pronoun/possessor. رنگ نیکو گرفت uses گرفت ('to take/receive') with رنگ نیکو as the thing taken — 'took on a good/fine color.' Word order places the predicate after the possessed phrase in typical poetic inversion.
segments.s531-g85: همی is an adverb ('ever/continually' or a narrative 'then/soon') placed before the predicate. تنگ شد is 'became tight/narrow' (شد = become). دوکدان is the noun subject ('spinning‑house' / cocoon) and بر تنِش is the locative phrase 'upon his body' (بر + تن + possessive enclitic ش = 'his'). The clause thus means 'the cocoon became tight on his body.'
segments.s531-g86: چو here introduces a simile/comparison ('as' / 'like'); مشک سیه گشت is 'became black like musk' (گشت = 'became/turned'). پیراهنش is the possessed noun 'his shirt' (پیراهن + enclitic ش). Word order is poetic: simile-marker + predicate + possessed noun.
segments.s531-g87: به مشک is an instrumental/locative phrase 'with/by musk' or 'perfumed with musk' (به used adverbially); اندرون پیکر is a genitival/compound expression 'the inner part of the body' (اندرون = inside, پیکر = body); زعفران follows as a predicate/adjective 'saffron-colored' modifying پیکر. The short clause stacks sensory predicates: 'inside (he) musk, (his) body saffron.'
segments.s531-g88: برو here is an older contracted form (effectively 'بر او' = 'on his') followed by پشت او 'his back.' از کران تا کران is a standard prepositional range 'from edge to edge' (از = from, تا = to/as far as, کران = edge). Together: 'on his back from edge to edge.'
segments.s531-g97: بهانه (pretext) is the direct object/topic 'a pretext'; همی is the archaic/historical-present adverbial marker indicating habitual or continuous action ('kept/was repeatedly'); ساخت is the past/epic tense of ساختن 'to make' (here ’made/put up’). بر + proper name (بر هفتواد) marks the target of the action ('against/to Haftvād'). The whole segment therefore reads 'kept making pretexts against Haftvād.'
segments.s531-g98: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result here ('so that / in order that'). دینار is the object 'dinar(s)'. بستاند is an old/epic-form verb (present/subjunctive/optative third person singular) expressing intended or potential action — 'may take / would exact'; its lexical sense (to seize/take) is not present in the provided sense-list. از marks the source/starting point 'from', and بدنژاد is an adjective/noun phrase 'the low‑born/ignoble'. The clause means 'so that he might take dinars from the low‑born.'
segments.s531-g99: ازان آگهی is a circumstantial phrase 'on/after that news' (ازان = 'from/at that'); مرد here denotes 'a man/person' (an unspecified man) and شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/rose' — together used idiomatically as 'a man rose up' or 'someone sprang up'. در نهیب gives the manner/place phrase 'in a (thundering) cry/rebuke' — نهیب is a loud, denunciatory cry. The segment thus conveys that upon hearing the news someone sprang up and cried aloud.
segments.s531-g100: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (here 'came forth' or 'appeared'). ازان شهر is the source phrase 'from that city'. دل با شکیب is an ablative/tributive phrase 'a heart with patience' i.e. 'a patient‑hearted man' — دل here stands for the person's heart/feeling and شکیب (archaic adjective/noun) means 'patient/enduring'. The segment describes a (patient‑hearted) person coming out of that city.
segments.s531-g101: همان qualifies 'هفت فرزند' as 'those same seven children/sons' (demonstrative/identifying). پیش here means 'in front of/before' and اندرون means 'the inner quarters/inner chamber'. Word order is demonstrative + numeral + noun + locative, so the phrase reads 'those same seven children before the inner chamber.'
segments.s531-g102: پر از ... is the fixed pattern 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'). درد دل is a genitive/compound meaning 'heartache / sorrow' (درد = pain, دل = heart). دیدگان پر ز خون repeats the 'full of' pattern (دیدگان = 'the eyes'; پر ز خون = 'full of blood', where ز is the poetic form of 'از'). Together the line attributes to those children 'hearts full of grief and eyes full of blood.'
segments.s531-g103: ز هر سو is the adverbial phrase 'from every side' (ز = poetic 'from', هر = 'every', سو = 'side/toward'). برانگیخت is the past tense 'arose/roused' (subject implied: voices/people), and بانگ و نفیر are the coordinated nouns 'cry/shout and bugle‑blast/alarm'. The segment reports that shouts and alarms rose up from all directions.
segments.s531-g104: برو here is an archaic connective/temporal particle meaning roughly 'thereupon' or 'then' in epic style (not a literal imperative 'go' in this context). انجمن گشت uses گشت (past of گشتن) with انجمن 'assembly' to mean 'an assembly was formed / people gathered'. برنا و پیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'young and old' describing those who gathered. Overall: 'thereupon an assembly—young and old—was formed.'
segments.s531-g57: من is the first-person subject 'I'. از here marks source or instrument ('by/from'), so از اختر کرم = 'by the star's generosity' (اختر + کرم = genitive: 'star's generosity'). چندان is an adverb modifying طراز, meaning 'so much / such', and طراز is a noun 'pattern / ornament' that will be completed by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s531-g58: بریسم is a 1st-person verbal form ('I embroider / I make'), here expressing the speaker's action. که introduces a result or purpose clause ('so that / that'). نیزم is orthographically odd here (appears as نیز/نیزم) but functions as the possessor/affected party of نیاز (understood as 'my need' or 'my being in need'); نیاید is a negative present/optative ('does not / will not come'), and نیاز is the noun 'need'—together نیزم نیاید نیاز = 'that my need will not come' (i.e. 'I will not be in need').
segments.s531-g59: به + رشت: به introduces the locative/relational phrase and رشت here is an archaic/past-form usage related to sewing/embroidering (an adjectival/past sense: 'that was sewn'). آنکجا 'there/where', برده is the participle 'taken/placed', بد 'bad', and پیش ازین = 'before this / previously'. The clause says roughly 'in that sewing which had been placed there it was bad before'.
segments.s531-g60: به کار آمدی‌گر is a conditional construction: به کار آمدی‌گر = 'if/when it should come to use' (آمدی‌گر is the '-گر' conditional/optative formation). بدی is the noun 'evil/harm', بیش = 'more', and از این = 'than before' (از + ین). The clause gives the result: 'if it were used, the harm would be greater than before.'
segments.s531-g61: سوی خانه برد is literally 'carried toward the house' (سوی = toward, خانه = house, برد here as a verbal root 'brought/carried'). آن طرازی = 'that ornament/ornamentation' (آن = that, طرازی = ornamentation) and که introduces a relative clause 'which...'. رشت is the same archaic/past sewing sense (i.e. 'which was/ had been sewn'). The clause as a whole: 'He carried that ornament toward the house that he had sewn.'
segments.s531-g62: دل مام او شد is straightforward: دل = 'heart', مام = 'mother' (here 'wet-nurse' or nurse), او = 'her', شد = 'became'. چو = 'as/like', خرم = 'joyful/pleasant', بهشت = 'paradise/garden'. The simile reads 'her wet-nurse's heart became like a joyful paradise.'
segments.s531-g63: همی is a frequentive/emphatic adverb here ('always / continually'). لختکی is a diminutive/qualifier 'a little / a small bit' (used here to qualify سیب), سیب = 'apple', and هر بامداد = 'every morning'. Word order is adverbial + quantified object + temporal phrase: 'always a small apple every morning.'
segments.s531-g64: پری‌روی دختر = 'the fairy‑faced maiden' (پری‌روی = fairylike-faced, دختر = maiden/daughter). بران is a deictic/adverbial 'to that one / on that' (here functioning like 'to her' or 'for that [purpose]'), کرم = 'generosity/kindness', and داد is the past verb 'gave'. Together: 'the fairy‑faced maiden gave that small apple as a kindness.'
segments.s531-g9: 'به یک روی' is a locative phrase meaning 'on one side' or 'on one face'; 'نزدیک او' means 'near him' and follows the locative phrase. 'بود' is the past copula 'was', and 'کوه' (the subject) appears at the end — an archaic word order where the verb precedes the noun it links to.
segments.s531-g10: 'شدندی' is an archaic past plural verb 'they became / they were made'; it governs the following noun phrase. 'همه دختران' means 'all the maidens' and 'همگروه' qualifies them as forming a single group or company.
segments.s531-g11: 'ازان هر یکی' is an old-fashioned ordering meaning 'from each (of them), one'. 'پنبه بردی' uses the past verb 'بردی' (took/carried up); here it describes taking cotton. 'به سنگ' indicates direction/placement 'onto a stone' (به = onto/toward, سنگ = stone).
segments.s531-g12: 'یکی' here means 'one (other/another)'. 'دوکدانی' is a spinning/ spindle-related noun (a spindle or spindle-case / small spinning apparatus). 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'of/from', and 'چوب خدنگ' means 'wood of khadang' — i.e. a spindle made from that wood; the phrase describes material (made from khadang-wood).
segments.s531-g13: 'به دروازه' is locative 'at the gate'. 'دختر' (here plural by sense/context) and the past verb 'شدی' ('became' / 'were made') indicate that the maidens formed into 'همگروه' — i.e. they became a single company or group. The verb form is archaic but functions as a past marker.
segments.s531-g14: 'خرامان' is an adverb meaning 'with a proud/stately gait'. 'ازین شهر' ('از' + 'ین' = 'from this city') gives the origin; 'تا پیش کوه' uses 'تا' = 'as far as' and 'پیش' = 'before/in front of', so the phrase means they strode from this city up to the front of the mountain.
segments.s531-g15: 'برآمیختندی' is an archaic past plural verb meaning 'they mixed together' (the prefix بر- plus آمیختن). 'خورشها' is the plural 'the stews/dishes' and 'بهم' means 'together' or 'into one', so the clause says the dishes were mingled together.
segments.s531-g16: 'نبودی' is the negative past copula 'there was not / there was no'. 'به خورد اندرون' literally 'for eating inside' (به = for/toward, خورد = eating / food to be eaten, اندرون = inside). 'بیش و کم' is an idiom 'more and less' meaning 'whether much or little', so the clause states there was nothing inside to eat in any quantity.
segments.s531-g33: به ره بر بدید is a compact, poetic locative + verb sequence: به (preposition) + ره (way) + بر (locative/onto) + بدید (past of دیدن). بدید is third-person past 'saw' or 'came in sight' and here functions as the main past verb governing the preceding place phrase. سبک (literally 'light') modifies the following verb برگرفت (بر + گرفت 'took up') and is adverbial: سبک برگرفت = 'took up lightly / set off lightly'. برگرفت is past tense (he/she took up/assumed); برگ- is an inseparable verbal element meaning upward/taking up.
segments.s531-g34: ز من بشنو is an imperative request: ز (poetic 'from') + من (object 'me') + بشنو (imperative of شنیدن 'listen/hear'). The order 'ز من بشنو' ('hear from me') is the usual poetic order with the prepositional source before the imperative. این داستان شگفت is a noun phrase 'this wondrous tale' with the adjective شگفت following the noun in a poetic adjective-after-noun placement.
segments.s531-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن خوب رخ is a nominal phrase where آن = 'that' and خوب رخ = 'fair/beautiful-faced' (خوب modifies رخ). میوه اندرگزید is the verb phrase: اندرگزید is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'chose/picked' (اندر- is an old prefix = 'in/into' and گزید is past of گزیدن 'choose'). Word order is standard for classical Persian: conjunction + subject phrase + verb.
segments.s531-g36: یکی functions as an indefinite numeral 'one' (a single fruit). در میان = 'in the middle' (preposition + noun). کرم آگنده is a noun + postpositive adjective construction common in poetry: کرم (worms/grubs) آگنده ('filled/laden with') — literally 'laden with worms.' دید is the past-tense verb 'saw'. The whole segment is a compact clause: 'she saw one (fruit) in the middle full of worms.'
segments.s531-g37: به انگشت here uses به in an instrumental/circumstantial sense ('with' or 'by'), followed by انگشت 'finger' — a terse poetic way to say 'with [her] finger'. زان = ز + آن 'from that' (meaning 'from it' or 'from that [branch/fruit]'). سیب is the noun 'apple'. برداشتش is past 'picked up / took it' (برداشت 'took up' with the enclitic -ش 'it' attached). The verb is third-person past; the enclitic object indicates the apple as its direct object.
segments.s531-g38: بدان is the compound of ب (to/onto) + آن (that) and functions as a locative: 'onto that / to that'. دوکدان is a compound noun 'spinning-house / distaff place' (a textile-work space or implement). نرم is an adjective 'soft' describing the place/cloth. بگذاشتش is the past form of گذاشتن with the prefix ب- (common in older/poetic forms) meaning 'placed / put', with the enclitic -ش 'it' (placed it). The sequence means 'she placed it on that soft distaff.'
segments.s531-g39: چو again = 'when'. برداشت is past 'took/drew' (here 'took out' or 'pulled off' cotton). زان دوکدان پنبه is the source phrase: زان = 'from that', دوکدان 'spinning-house' (or place of spinning/spindle) and پنبه 'cotton' (object). گفت is the past tense 'said'. Note the poetic word order places the object phrase before the reporting verb; overall: 'when she took the cotton from that spinning-house, she said...'.
segments.s531-g40: به نام is a fixed devotional formula 'in the name (of)'. خداوند is the vocative/nominative 'God' or 'the Lord' and is followed by epithets بی‌یار و جفت (joined by و). In classical/poetic usage these epithets are postpositive and condensed; بی‌یار literally 'without a friend' or 'unsupported' is here used poetically to mean 'without peer' or 'matchless', and جفت ('pair/match') likewise participates in the idiom emphasizing uniqueness. The whole phrase is an invocation: 'In the name of God, the peerless Lord.'
segments.s531-g73: همان + کرم + فرخ form a noun phrase: همان 'that/the same' precedes the noun کرم (here 'worm'), and فرخ is an attributive adjective 'fortunate/auspicious' placed after the noun in classical style. بدیشان is the dative pronoun 'to them' and نمود is a past‑tense, literary verb meaning 'appeared/showed' (3rd person singular). The clause is a simple past statement: 'That same fortunate worm appeared to them.'
segments.s531-g74: زن و شوی را is a coordinated, definite object/beneficiary marked by the object marker را (here 'the woman and the husband' / 'the wife and husband'). روشنایی is a noun 'light/brightness' and فزود is a past tense verb 'increased/gave' (3rd person singular). The clause means roughly 'it gave light to the woman and her husband.'
segments.s531-g75: به فالی is a prepositional phrase 'as a divination/omen' (به + فالی) modifying the verb گرفت. آن سخن is the object 'that speech/word(s)', and هفتواد is the proper name (Haftvād) acting as the subject; گرفت is the past tense 'took/accepted'. The clause: 'Haftvād took that speech as an omen.'
segments.s531-g76: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. کاری here functions as 'any deed / any (such) action' (the provided lexicon lacks the exact nominal sense, so mapped as -1). نکردی is the classical past negative (3rd‑person past negative in narrative) meaning 'did not do'. به دل نیز یاد is a small prepositional phrase 'also a remembrance in the heart' (به = in/to, دل = heart, نیز = also, یاد = memory). Together the clause says he did not do any action that would leave a remembrance in his heart.
segments.s531-g77: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. تا here is temporal 'when/until' introducing the time clause; برآمد is a past verb 'arose/came about'. برین is composed of بر + ین (an archaic/poetic form meaning 'that/this upon'), forming the phrase برین روزگار 'that time/at that time' (literally 'upon that time').
segments.s531-g78: فروزنده‌تر is the comparative form of the adjective 'فروزنده' ('more radiant' or 'more illuminating') formed with the suffix -تر. گشت is a past tense copular/transformative verb 'became'. هر روز کار is a noun phrase 'each day's work/affair' (هر = each/every modifying روز کار). The predicate structure is '... became more radiant.'
segments.s531-g79: مگر introduces an exception/conditional sense here ('only/except/was it not that'). ز اختر کرم is a genitive phrase 'from the star of grace' (ز = from/of, اختر = star, کرم = grace/generosity). گفتی is the past tense verb 'spoke/said' in this context (the provided senses list does not give this verbal sense, so marked -1). سخن is the object 'a word/words'. The clause asks/claims that it was by the star's utterance that something happened.
segments.s531-g80: برو here functions as a connective/particle 'thereupon/then' (an archaic usage not captured by the simple 'go' senses, so marked -1). نو is the adjective 'new', شدی is the past form 'became' (3rd person past), and روزگار کهن is the noun phrase 'the ancient/old time' (روزگار = time/age, کهن = old/ancient). The clause: 'thereupon the ancient age became new.'
segments.s531-g41: Subject + temporal phrase followed by a prepositional phrase: من امروز ("I today") gives the actor and time. بر + اختر کرم سیب is a prepositional phrase literally "on/upon the star of (the) apple’s generosity"; in classical poetic word order the ezāfe linking کرم and سیب is implied rather than spelled out, so the phrase means "under the favorable/benign star associated with the apple."
segments.s531-g42: به + verbal noun رشتن gives purpose/infinitive "to spin; for spinning," and نمایم is 1st‑person singular (poetic/final‑vowel form) meaning "I will show / cause." شما را is the object with the postposed object marker را. نهیب is used elliptically here as the notion "rebuke" (in poetry it can stand as noun or compressed verbal idea), so the whole clause reads "I will show (you) to spin, (and) I will rebuke you."
segments.s531-g43: همه دختران is the plural subject "all the girls/maidens." Two adjectival predicates, شاد و خندان, are joined by و and follow the subject; شدند is the past plural of شدن meaning "became," so the clause states a change of state: "all the girls became happy and smiling."
segments.s531-g44: This is a continuation of the predicate: two descriptive phrases (گشاده‌رخ = "broad‑faced/open‑faced") and سیم دندان ("silver‑toothed/bright‑toothed") joined by و, with شدند repeated, so "they became open‑faced and silver‑toothed." Note سیم ("silver/bright") and دندان form a compound describing teeth; دندان here is the noun "teeth."
segments.s531-g45: دو چندان ("twofold / twice as much") sets up a comparison introduced by که. رشتی is an agentive/byname meaning "a person from Rasht" (here: the spinner from Rasht). به روزی is an adverbial phrase "in a day / per day" (به + روزی) and برشت is the past form meaning "he spun/was spun" — so the clause reads "twice as much as the Rashti spun in a day."
segments.s531-g46: شمارش ("his counting/tally") is the object; همی is a frequentative/adverbial particle meaning "customarily/still/continually." بر زمین is the locative "on the ground," and برنوشت here functions as a past/habitual verb "he would write down / he wrote down." Together: "he would write down his tally on the ground."
segments.s531-g47: وزانجا is a fused locative/conjunctive adverb "and from there." بیامد is simple past "he came." به کردار دود is a simile built with به + کردار ("in the manner/way") + دود ("smoke") — literally "he came in the manner of smoke," i.e. swiftly or like a wisp of smoke.
segments.s531-g48: به مادر نمود is "he showed (it) to the mother" (نمود as past of نِمودن/نمودن = "showed/appeared"). The subordinate clause آن کجا رشته بود is a reported locative: آن ("that/the"), کجا ("where"), رشته بود (رشته = "thread/place of spinning" and بود = past copula "was"), so the full idea is "he showed the mother where the thread/spinning had been / where he had been spinning."
segments.s531-g49: برو is an imperative 'go' placed before the reporting verb as an interjection/quoted imperative. آفرین here is the acclamation 'bravo' used as the object of the verb کرد (past tense 'did/made' used idiomatically as 'exclaimed/praised'). مادر is the subject which follows the verb (poetic verb‑subject order). به مهر is a prepositional phrase 'with affection' modifying آفرین کرد.
segments.s531-g50: که introduces the subordinate content/reason clause ('for/that'). برخوردی here is used as a perfective past form meaning 'you came/you originate' (classical usage), with از marking source 'from'. مادر‌ای is 'a mother' with ای functioning as an emphatic/vocative element attached to مادر or simply as poetic padding; خوب‌چهر is the adjective 'fair‑faced'. The whole clause explains/reasons why the mother praised.
segments.s531-g51: به introduces a temporal/locative phrase 'at' or 'by'; شبگیر is a nocturnal/time‑of‑night word ('nightfall'/'nocturnal'). چون is a simile particle 'like', followed by ریسمان 'rope(s)'. برشمرد is the past tense verb 'he counted/enumerated' placed at the end, with the subject implied from context.
segments.s531-g52: دو چندانک = 'doubled' (دو چندان) with the enclitic/resultive -ک/چنانک introducing the result ('so that/to the degree that'). هر بار = 'each time/whenever'. بردی is a past or habitual form 'you carried/took', while ببرد (with prefixed بـ) is a subjunctive/optative or habitual form 'you would carry/should carry' — the pair emphasizes repeated/resultant action (typical poetic repetition).
segments.s531-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is the past 'came'. The following ب + دان (here written separately) functions as 'to that' (بدان = 'to that'), pointing to the place/occasion; چاره‌جوی انجمن is the noun phrase 'the seeker of remedy to the assembly' (چاره‌جوی modifies انجمن or is appositive), so the whole phrase means 'when the seeker of remedy came to that assembly.'
segments.s531-g54: به رشتن is an infinitival/gerundial phrase 'to/for spinning' (رشتن = to spin/weave). نهاده is the past participle/verb 'placed/put' (i.e., 'set to'), and دل و گوش و تن is the enumerated object list 'heart and ear and body' — the things he devoted/set to the spinning/effort. Word order places the verbal phrase before its objects (poetic style).
segments.s531-g55: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت, the past verb 'he said/spoke'. با introduces the indirect object 'to/with' and نامور دختران is the addressed group 'renowned maidens' (دختران = maidens/daughters). The verb precedes the indirect object in this poetic ordering.
segments.s531-g56: که‌ای here functions as a vocative marker introducing direct address (equivalent to 'O ...'). ماه‌رویان is a plural adjective‑noun 'moon‑faced ones' (i.e. beautiful faces) and نیک‌اختران is a compound meaning 'good/auspicious‑starred ones' or 'fortunate ones'; و simply connects the two epithets being used in the direct address.
segments.s531-g1: ببین is an imperative 'look/see' addressing the listener; این شگفتی is its direct object 'this wonder'. که introduces a relative/clausal content following and links to the reported speech. دهقان (subject) + چه (interrogative 'what') + گفت (past 'said') forms the subordinate clause 'what the peasant said' — word order is subject (دهقان) before the interrogative (چه) and the verb (گفت).
segments.s531-g2: بدانگه is an adverb 'thereupon/at that moment' linking this action to the previous clause. که again introduces the following clause. بگشاد here is an archaic/poetic past verb 'opened/revealed' (the listed senses don't show the verbal meaning). راز از نهفت is a noun phrase 'a secret from concealment' where از is the preposition 'from' and نهفت means 'hidden/concealed'; together they mean 'revealed the secret that was hidden.'
segments.s531-g3: Two prepositional phrases headed by به give destinations/targets: به شهر کجاران 'to the town(s) of Kajaran' and به دریای پارس 'to the sea of Pars'. دریای پارس is a genitival construction ('the sea of Pars'). The repeated به simply marks the two parallel directions.
segments.s531-g4: چه introduces a rhetorical/questioning element 'what'. گوید is the verb 'says/one may say' (an archaic/present form here — the listed senses omit the verbal meaning). ز is the poetic preposition 'of/about' marking the topic, so ز بالا و پهنای پارس means 'concerning the height(s) and breadth of Pars'; بالا is 'high/height' and پهنای is the noun 'breadth/width'.
segments.s531-g5: یکی before شهر is the indefinite 'a certain/one' city. شهر بد تنگ is an adjectival phrase: بد here functions as an adverbial/intensifying element 'badly/very' modifying تنگ 'narrow/cramped'. و connects the next phrase; مردم بسی places 'the people' followed by بسی (classical 'much/many') indicating large numbers.
segments.s531-g6: This clause gives the consequence/cause: ز marks cause 'from/because of' (ز کوشش = 'from toil/effort'), کوشش is 'effort/toil', بدی is 'evil/misfortune' (object), and خوردن here is used in the sense 'to receive/suffer' (not literal eating). هر کسی at the end means 'every person' or 'everyone' — the order places the affected people after the verb phrase, a common classical word order.
segments.s531-g7: بدان is the fused locative/demonstrative 'in/at that' (here given as ب + دان); it points back to 'that city'. شهر 'city' is the noun, دختر فراوان 'many maidens' is the predicative phrase where فراوان 'plentiful' follows the noun. The final بدی in this sentence is an archaic past/copular form meaning 'there were/there was' (the provided senses do not list this verbal/copular meaning).
segments.s531-g8: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/whereby'. بی‌کام is an adjective 'unfulfilled/unsuccessful'. جوینده نان is a compound noun phrase 'one who seeks bread' (جوینده 'seeker', نان here used figuratively as 'livelihood'). The terminal بدی again functions as an archaic past/copular 'was/remained' (sense not listed), meaning 'was left unfulfilled'.
segments.s531-g17: نرفتی is a negative past-form used impersonally/figuratively here — literally ‘did not go (away)’ meaning ‘did not cease’ or ‘there was no end’. سخن گفتن is a verbal noun + infinitive construction meaning ‘talking/speaking’; از introduces the topic ‘about/concerning’ and links to the two nouns خواب (sleep) and خورد (here ‘eating/food’). Word order is inverted/poetic: the verb نرفتی precedes the verbal noun phrase.
segments.s531-g18: ازان = از + آن 'from that/of that' indicating source or cause. پنبه‌شان is a possessed noun 'their cotton/wadding' (پنبه + possessive -شان). بود is the past copula 'was/there was'. ننگ و نبرد form the predicate 'shame and strife/battle'; the line literally reads 'from that their cotton was shame and battle' → 'from that cotton of theirs came shame and conflict' (poetic inversion: source phrase fronted).
segments.s531-g19: شدندی is an archaic past plural 'they became / they turned' (verb with an older plural ending). شبانگه is an adverbial noun 'at night / nightfall'. سوی خانه باز = 'toward the house again / back toward the house' where سوی + noun marks direction and باز functions as an adverb 'back/again'. The verb is fronted, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s531-g20: شده is the perfect/participial form 'has become / was made'. پنبه‌شان 'their cotton' is the subject/material; ریسمان 'rope' is the resulting noun; طراز here names the maker/type — used as an attributive noun meaning a craftsman/maker (poetic concision). The phrase means 'their cotton had been turned into a rope (by a maker)'.
segments.s531-g21: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) 'in/at that'. شهر is the noun 'city'. بی‌چیز و خرم are two adjectives applied to what was placed: بی‌چیز 'poor/needy' and خرم 'cheerful/fruitful/thriving'. نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'placed/installed' (here transitive past), with the (grammatical) subject omitted and the person placed given in the following segment. The sequence shows poetic fronting of the locative/object phrase before the verb.
segments.s531-g22: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / someone', مرد 'man'. بد نام is a compound meaning 'ill-named / ill-reputed / notorious' (بد modifies نام). او is the third-person pronoun 'he' and هفتواد is a proper name in apposition; the copula is omitted, so the phrase reads 'a certain man, ill-reputed — he (was) Haftvād.'
segments.s531-g23: برین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this way / thus'. بر نام او literally 'on his name' = 'concerning his reputation'. از چه is the interrogative 'from what / by what means / why'. رفت is the past of رفتن 'went', used idiomatically here meaning 'came about / happened' (literal 'went' used in the sense 'came to be'). The whole segment asks 'Thus, from what did his fame/reputation arise?'.
segments.s531-g24: ازیراک is a proper name (Azirak). او را is the dative/possessive construction 'to him / for him' (او + object marker را) used to show possession in classical Persian; پسر بود هفت literally 'sons were seven' — the numeral often follows the copula in classical order, so the clause means 'he had seven sons.' The past copula بود confirms the past possession.

sentences.s531-s45: A certain clean chest he made black; inside it he fashioned a compartment/place.
sentences.s531-s46: So it came about that in the city of the kinless no one spoke a word about injustice or justice.
sentences.s531-s47: Honor and reverence and possessions came to him on high, and those seven children too became wealthy.
sentences.s531-s48: There was a certain bad mir in that town — lofty in appearance, with an army and show and scent.
sentences.s531-s53: Wherever a dinar had to be paid, he gave the excavators many things.
sentences.s531-s54: An army was raised against him; the entire assembly were renowned swordsmen.
sentences.s531-s55: All of them at once went before his son and became warriors.
sentences.s531-s13: He had only one beloved daughter, and that was enough; you could not (readily) count his daughters for anyone.
sentences.s531-s14: So it happened that one day all the companions sat with their spindles before the mountain.
sentences.s531-s15: They mixed it where they had it; when the stew was ready they set down the spindle.
sentences.s531-s16: So it happened that this fortunate girl—an apple once fell from the tree.
sentences.s531-s33: However much you added to that cotton, the maiden P̣arfuson still grew (on).
sentences.s531-s34: So it happened that one day mother and father spoke with the accomplished daughter.
sentences.s531-s35: They asked, 'Did you, O pure-bodied sister, perhaps take many Brisi — or rather, (take) a fairy?'
sentences.s531-s36: Light of figure and silver-hued, she told her mother that she had hidden that apple and the little worm inside.
sentences.s531-s41: They would not let this worm be treated with contempt; they kept feeding it well.
sentences.s531-s42: That worm became strong-bodied and gained strength, and its head and back took on a fine color.
sentences.s531-s43: The spinning‑house (cocoon) grew tight around his body, and his shirt turned black as musk.
sentences.s531-s44: Inside he was perfumed with musk, his body saffron-colored, and his back saffron from edge to edge.
sentences.s531-s49: He continually made pretexts against Haftvād, so that he might exact dinars from the low‑born.
sentences.s531-s50: On hearing that news, a man rose up with a thunderous cry; from that city came a patient‑hearted man.
sentences.s531-s51: Those same seven children stood before the inner chamber, hearts full of sorrow and eyes full of blood.
sentences.s531-s52: From every side arose cries and bugle‑blasts; thereupon an assembly gathered, young and old.
sentences.s531-s29: By the star's generosity I will embroider such a design that my need will not come.
sentences.s531-s30: Where she had embroidered before it was badly done; if it should come to use, the harm would be greater than before.
sentences.s531-s31: He carried that ornament toward the house which he had embroidered; his wet-nurse's heart became like a joyful paradise.
sentences.s531-s32: Every morning the fairy‑faced maiden would give that small apple as a kindness.
sentences.s531-s5: On one side, near him, there was a mountain; all the maidens were gathered together as a company.
sentences.s531-s6: From each of them one took cotton onto a stone; another had a spindle (or spindle-case) made of khadang wood.
sentences.s531-s7: At the gate the maidens, gathered as a band, strode proudly from this city to before the mountain.
sentences.s531-s8: They mixed the dishes together; inside there was nothing to eat, neither much nor little.
sentences.s531-s17: He took the road and lightly set off; hear from me this wondrous tale.
sentences.s531-s18: When that fair-faced one chose a fruit, she saw one in the middle full of worms.
sentences.s531-s19: With her finger she plucked that apple, and placed it upon the soft distaff.
sentences.s531-s20: When she drew (took) the cotton from that spinning-house, she said, 'In the name of God, the peerless Lord.'
sentences.s531-s37: That same lucky worm showed itself to them and bestowed light upon the woman and her husband.
sentences.s531-s38: Haftvād took those words as a divination; he did not perform any deed that would plant a remembrance in the heart.
sentences.s531-s39: Thus, when that time arose, each day's affairs became more and more radiant.
sentences.s531-s40: Only when the star of grace spoke a word did the ancient age become renewed.
sentences.s531-s21: Today I will, beneath the star of apple’s kindness, show you how to spin — and I will rebuke you.
sentences.s531-s22: All the girls became joyful and smiling; they became open‑faced and with bright (silver) teeth.
sentences.s531-s23: It was twice as much as the Rasht‑spinner had spun in a day; he would write down his tally on the ground.
sentences.s531-s24: And from there he came like smoke; he showed the mother where he had been spinning.
sentences.s531-s25: The mother, with affection, praised him, saying, "Go — bravo, for you have come from a fair‑faced mother."
sentences.s531-s26: At nightfall he counted them like ropes, doubling them so that whenever you carried a load you would carry twice as much.
sentences.s531-s27: When that seeker of a remedy came to the assembly, he had set heart and ear and body to spinning.
sentences.s531-s28: Thus he spoke to the renowned maidens: 'O moon‑faced and auspicious‑starred ones!'
sentences.s531-s1: Behold this wonder: what the peasant said, and thereupon how he disclosed a secret that had been hidden.
sentences.s531-s2: To the towns of Kajaran and to the sea of Pars — what can be said of Pars's heights and breadth?
sentences.s531-s3: In one city the place was very cramped and the people numerous; from hard toil every person suffered misfortune.
sentences.s531-s4: In that city there were many maidens, so that the seekers of bread remained unfulfilled.
sentences.s531-s9: There was no end to talk about sleep and food; from that cotton of theirs came shame and strife.
sentences.s531-s10: At nightfall they turned back toward the house; their cotton had become a rope made by a craftsman.
sentences.s531-s11: In that city he set up (or placed) someone poor yet thriving — a man of ill repute: Haftvād.
sentences.s531-s12: Thus — from what did his name arise? Azirak had seven sons.
